speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6 of the interview with Tola Hauptman. You were talking about going to find your brother. You knew where he was, and you told us about what happened to him. What happened to him, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened once you-- When I came, he was a like a stranger to me. And as I said, he was-- he's-- he's 5 foot 9' or 10'-- no, he was 5 foot 10' and a walking skeleton. And we didn't even kiss. I-- I felt sort of-- I couldn't kiss him. You know, it was a walking death.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, what happened to-- to you? He scarcely answered. He didn't want even to talk, you know? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took him with me. [? And my ?] [? truth ?] [? is ?] that that was Mrs. [? Tokarska ?] across the road. I make big friendship with her. She was an elderly woman who had-- really, to think about it, they were most [LAUGHS] different stories, you know? And-- unusual stories. That's what I wanted to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had two sons. One was somehow with the army on-- on a ship, and he went to Canada, leaving behind a wife who was a gypsy. And she died very soon. That was during the war. I don't know. I didn't know her. But they were four children from that gypsy and [? Tokarska ?] marriage, which were-- one was nicer than the other. Beautiful kids. And Mrs. [? Tokarska ?] was a sweet woman, except that she didn't like Jews because the Jews killed the Christ. You know, that was her reason. Because she had another son who was-- who had some sort of a shop, and he probably wasn't successful or something. And the whole-- [INAUDIBLE], the whole-- it was the-- the result of the Jews. The Jews didn't let him sort of be successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she blamed the Jews for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She blamed the Jews. And she had all sorts of Bibles and drawings and-- and writings. Everything was against the Jews. But to me, she was wonderful. She believed this story. And I said, I've got a younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she didn't know you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And she-- [LAUGHS] having-- having four gypsies, she couldn't recognize that I am Jewish. You know? And I said, I've got a younger brother, and he was sick. I left him, because I came to the wedding that was-- she knew that I came to Krystyna's wedding. So I have to fetch him. He is very sick. And don't be frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he-- he needs some home for a short time until we know what happened to our parents. Because-- and that was true that the parents were in Warsaw, where the uprising was. You know? But, of course, I didn't count that my parents are alive. You know? So she said, all right, bring him. We'll see what we can do. Because the place where I was, I was already quite unwelcome, but I-- I was trying my best to help and everything. And they sort of, you know, didn't tell me to go, but, also, when the Russians came, she said, remember, [? Irene, ?] you're not going to-- to greet the Russians because I throw you out. I said, I-- I haven't got the slightest idea what they are and where they are. I'm happy to be free. And that's all I can say. And that was it. They hated the Russians, I think, more than the Germans. But, anyway, I took him, the skeleton. I was holding him on my back. You know how many kilometers it might have been? 15 or 16 kilometers through the forest. And this-- it wasn't any communication. And people were saying to me if I take him to the hospital. I didn't say anything. I was just, you know, schlepping him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I brought him there. She looked after him. She might have bought for him. He-- he had hemorrhoids because from a long sitting. And-- and I was afraid she would-- she didn't recognize. He didn't look Jewish at all. And she didn't recognize me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was the-- you know, quite happy, and slowly he came to himself. You know? But what I found out, when he left the Russians, maybe they were the same Russians who were interrogated them and-- and my-- I don't think so, because they were officials. But some Russians were, you know, stealing the Polish goods. And they came one night, you know, and wanted to steal the goods from them. They were rich people. This was the Freunds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Freunds. And Mrs. Freund started to scream. And one of the Russians said, you don't scream because we'll kill you. And she started to scream because the-- everything was very precious to-- to her-- the-- the furs and the jewelry and so on. And they killed her. And they killed her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was really very sorry, because, good or bad about my brother's survival. Soon after, Mr. Freund died. And that was a terrible thing, but he couldn't live without her. He loved her very much. But the thing is-- that that was when the parents were back. The thing is, when my mother approached the family and said that my brother will have a speech, they said, no. None of you is going to the funeral. That was already after the war. So, in other words, they-- even those who were doing the good things were afraid of the others, that they will point them out that they helped the Jews. Many of times did I hear, you still alive? The only good thing Hitler did that finish out the Jews, or killed the Jews. You know? So that was one thing. Afterwards, I met my two cousins. Only two cousins survived. Actually, three cousins. One was very little. And-- Was this after your parents came back, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberation of Praga, it still was-- the Russians were, you know, around Warsaw. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said you met your cousins? Was that before your parents came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before. I was-- you know, the Russians called me to-- to make the-- to make the-- you know, like on the frontier, you-- you dig the ground, you know, to make [INAUDIBLE], you know, against-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] against artillery or something so you can hide. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they called the private people, whoever was-- I couldn't-- I couldn't keep the-- the blooming thing, you  know? I was so weak. But, anyway, they only thing that they found my two cousins. And they said, come over. There is a Jewish family. And you'll be OK over there. As it came out, I wasn't OK. I would have been better off, you know, in the old place, because the Jewish family, they were the butchers from the same place where we used to live. And they were, you know, like-- they were always envious of the rich ones. You know? And they were very poor before the war. So-- so they had a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in us being homeless, and so they used us very much. And they made some monkey business, you know. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was it. So how long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: With them? Not long. Because my mother soon arrived. I forgot to tell you from the Slawinski. Can I go back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: A sin. It was a sin when it was-- oh, wait, it was the holy week. Easter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '43. '43. Easter. I remember. It couldn't be Christmas. It was Easter. And they invited people. The daughter, she insisted that they make a big party. You know? And I said, what about me? You have to be hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the one-- the daughter that was so cruel to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So cruel. And she was cruel all the time. And they were-- of course, they were all drunk like pigs. And I was three days and three nights under the bed. [INAUDIBLE] like that. They are drinking and singing and really rude and so on. And a little baby is with them, little, maybe two years old, girl or boy, I don't remember. And she's throwing a ball under the bed. And I thought she will come here, and she will tell her parents. So I throw the ball back, and she's throwing it again, because she-- it was a big fun for her. And then she's screaming, Mom! Mom! Look! Look! You know, but luckily, the mother was drunk, and she didn't notice. So that was one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another thing was this Slawinski-- the older son had a girlfriend, and she was coming every night to sleep. That was, mind you, everything it was in one room. You know? So they were-- there was the broken bed for me. When my mother came, we were both sleeping on the broken bed. And two beds for the younger brother and the older who had a girlfriend whose husband was in Germany. So in the meantime, she had a boyfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was-- she didn't talk to me. She was indifferent. But the younger brother wouldn't touch me because he hated the Jews, so I was lucky in that respect. But he had a girlfriend who was very low family. He was-- I don't know. He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some sort of a worker, but he was working for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: He--

speaker: interviewee
text: Plumber. Plumber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her father was a plumber.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. The youngest Slawinski boy had a girlfriend who was married, who was much older than him.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And She was having an affair with him. You see? And the plumber was quite rich. And, also, if he would find out about her, he would kill everyone. He was a-- a-- a animal. And it was at the same time when they came for Easter, I had to go to the toilet. And I closed myself. And-- or maybe it was another time. And I couldn't come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was, you know, knocking at the door. Who is there? Who is there? And-- and-- and the woman said, the neighbor, because her toilet is broken. And when he went away, I hide under the-- in the corridor under the thing, the coats, you know? And he didn't notice me. So that was one thing. But when the girlfriend came, she wanted to have affair with him, sleep with him. And I was hidden. That was that sort of-- you know, for a dressmaker, this sort of [NON-ENGLISH] to change your clothes. And I was behind the wardrobe and behind the wall and holding it. You know? So the boy didn't want to sleep with her, because he knew that I was there. He just couldn't. You know? But he couldn't tell her either. So she was getting mad. What's the matter with you? Don't you want to sleep with me? So he said, I can't. I just can't. I'm-- I'm sick. So she said, don't be silly. Someone must be here, she said, all of the sudden. And I thought, oh, that's bad. And I was just shrinking, you know, going down, but holding the-- the-- the thing. And she trying-- she was looking everywhere until she came to my place. And she was trying to take it back, and I was holding it. You know? I don't know how strong I was, but I was holding to it because I knew this is my end. And he said, leave it! Leave it! Didn't help. And we were struggling for a few minutes, and, finally, she gave up. You know? She went away. I don't know what it was. I don't know, you know, [? thing ?] like that. So, where were we? We were going-- we were-- you went to the family, the Jewish family, and your-- but your mother came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't recall very-- very good stay over there, and I was regretting it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother with you, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. I wouldn't leave my brother. And I was thinking about going to the army-- Polish or whatever army. It was Russian army. There were many Jews. And I thought, if I work in a hospital as a-- it was the cousins. We were-- we were planning, because we didn't have anything. You know. We had to eat. We didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, finally, I decided against it, because they wouldn't accept my brother. You saw-- you see? So I resigned from that. I didn't go. And soon my mother came, so I was really relieved. And where did your mother take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother stayed a few days with us. Then she took me to-- to our place. But our place was occupied already, and it was a-- a [INAUDIBLE] office, which gave you the right to be there, because you just can't-- even if it was yours, you can't go in, because people are living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had a funny experience with a Russian man who was talking into the tube, but no one was on the other end. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She wants her flat back. And give it to her straightaway. No one was on the other end. He didn't want to do anything. You know? And he didn't. So, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we took one room. And there were two more families. Can you imagine how crowded it was? Now, my mother, she was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I told you about her, she was in [? Grauhof, ?] but she-- the woman where she was, they sent Polish police and the Germans to get some money. They also knew, at that time, that the grandfather is alive and the grandfather has got a lot of money. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they said, give the money and give the address of your father-in-law. And my mother said, no. I don't know where he is. And they-- they shoot over her head. There is a place there still today. You know? And it was all combined with the Poles who were there. You know? They were-- And this was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this was before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I'm going back. How my brother survived. I don't know what I should talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just shortly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, really, it's your story, rather than how she survived. But a few details are fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few details. She went to a German woman who had a-- a interstate transport business, [? where it is. ?] And she was working as a house woman, a servant, cooking. She had a little dog. And the SS once were coming to the place, and there were quite a few truck drivers. And they were all, as I can see it now, probably in love with my mother. You know? And they said that whatever the German bitch will say, you tell us. We'll fix her. You know? That was it. And one day, she sees that someone is hiding there. You know, someone saw my mother and is hiding behind the door. And my mother understood that he's Jewish, and she said, don't be afraid. I'm to same. Came out that he was the German woman husband. And she was-- he was hidden in her place. And they caught him afterwards, and they torture him. They couldn't kill him, because he would not admit that he's a Jew. Probably changed his religion, because he had a German wife and so on. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We won't go into his story, because we're going back to your story. Let's-- let's talk about what happened once you're mother found you. And you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: When my mother found me, she said that my father should be alive, because she had a most unusual, unheard story, that because he was hidden under the-- from the cellar even deeper, well, well, they were-- his friends so-called-- Polish people-- he was hidden in their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when they left, when they-- when there was an uprising in Warsaw, everyone had to leave because it was a-- a fighting area. You know? No one was left. But my father didn't have options. They didn't tell him. And he was underground, from the cellar even deeper. Because I told you, I think, that they had hams and all sort of sausages. It was like freezing something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put all the goods and [? eiderdowns ?] and everything what had any value when they left the house. And my father was sleeping. He was-- he could only stay in a lying position. And he was like that. He didn't have anything to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother, at this time, was in Pruszkow as a servant working for someone else. It was a Dutch, Polish-Dutch. And she-- because she was so nice-looking and so on, she went there, and she said to the-- she was very badly fed also, because they were very mean, you know? She was working hard and just had enough to survive. So she was collecting a bit of food every month to get-- or every few weeks-- to get to my father. But she couldn't, because it was a front line. So she got that German officer, and she said, look, this is my house. I have to get some things, because I have nothing to live from. And he gave her a pass to go there. And she got there, and she brought him some lard, you know, some fat, and some bread, which he kept because he was like that. And what saved him, it-- it was the-- was-- with the sausage, they used garlic. It was a lot of garlic. So the-- the bread was bad, but he ate it piece by piece together with garlic. And he had a small bottle of vodka and one apple. And that apple, he kept. And all of a sudden-- that was a few times. Then she couldn't go anymore. All of a sudden, and-- and up at the top were all Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had to go to-- out to get fresh water. So what they did, they knew that someone is there. So they put cotton-- cotton around. If the cotton will be broken, it means that someone-- but my father was very careful. And under the cotton, he-- he-- in-- in such a way, he got out that no one noticed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to move with his story, because that's really his story.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. So this is nearing the end. But the thing is-- that-- this is the-- the unusual thing. That's why I'm telling you in a few words. He was caught over there by the people who were hiding my father. There is brother-in-law who brought the Germans to-- to steal this stuff. Can you imagine? And my father knew one thing. Once he will leave the-- the hiding place, they will kill him. So he was reasoning, if I stay here, they-- they don't want my blood to get over this stuff. So I'm safe here, so I'm not moving. So the Pole who knew my father, he knew from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't-- don't move your microphone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. He said, Strosberg, come out. No, he didn't say-- he knew his name. I'm talking about something. Come out. Nothing will-- no one will hurt you. And he said, that's what you're telling me. I know that the Germans will kill me. That was going for an hour. Finally, my father gave up, because he promised him. And he kept his word. He didn't-- he-- they said that they're going to take him to Pruszkow to the Polish camp. And he-- they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what the people-- my-- my mother was looking for him but didn't know where he is. So she met the people already who left the house on the way, because they were back. And they told her, you know what your husband is doing? He's a foreman. He is a driver on a coach. He's driving German SS men around, because my father knew-- and he loved horses. He knew horses. You know? So when my mother heard this, she said, it's insane. My-- my husband was out there [? at night, ?] and, you know, hiding all the time. All of a sudden, he's driving the Germans. That was impossible. We've now come to the end of this tape now, because we're going to start another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I-- I just say a few more words.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
